beats.text: Probability begins by naming everything that could happen, then assigning each event a number between zero and one.

visual.sub: Sample spaces, events, and three axioms

beats.text: The sample space is the set of all outcomes, and an event is any subset of it.
In 1933 Kolmogorov reduced the entire subject to three rules: non-negativity, total mass one, and additivity for disjoint events.
When events can overlap, summing double-counts the intersection, so the addition rule subtracts it back out once.
For finite equally-likely spaces, probability reduces to counting favorable outcomes over possible ones.

visual.note: Valid only when outcomes are equally likely

beats.text: With P of A at 0.5, P of B at 0.4, and overlap 0.2, the union is 0.7.
Conditioning revises a probability once you learn an event has occurred, shrinking the world down to that event.
The probability of A given B keeps only the part of A inside B, rescaled by the size of B.
The chain rule factors a joint probability, and averaging over a partition assembles a total probability.
Two events are independent when their joint probability factors into the product of their marginals.

visual.note: Independence is a property of the probabilities

beats.text: Conditioning is not symmetric: most rain comes with clouds, but most clouds bring no rain.
Rolling a fair die, conditioning on even raises the chance of being above three from 0.5 to about 0.667.
We can measure how often a cause produces evidence, but we want the reverse: the cause given the evidence.
Bayes scales the prior by how well the data fit the hypothesis, then renormalizes over all hypotheses.
In odds form, posterior odds are the prior odds times the likelihood ratio, the evidence acting as a multiplier.
Among ten thousand people with a rare disease, false positives outnumber true positives roughly five to one.
A test with 99 percent sensitivity and 95 percent specificity on a 1 percent disease is right only about 16.7 percent of the time it fires.
A strong test applied to a rare hypothesis still leaves the posterior small, because the multiplier acts on a number near zero.
A random variable attaches a number to each outcome, letting us do arithmetic with chance.

visual.sub: The center of mass and the spread

beats.text: Expectation is the probability-weighted average of the values the variable can take.
A fair die has expectation 3.5, a balance point it can never actually show.
Variance is the expected squared deviation from the mean, computed as the mean of the square minus the square of the mean.

visual.note: The same die has variance about 2.917

beats.text: The law of large numbers says the running average of independent draws converges to the true expectation.
Convergence is slow: error shrinks like one over root n, so halving it takes four times the data.

concepts.title: Pitfalls — base rates & the prosecutor's fallacy

beats.text: Probability's hardest errors are not algebraic but interpretive: the mind reaches for the wrong conditional.

visual.sub: Base rates and the prosecutor's fallacy

beats.text: Base-rate neglect quotes a 99 percent test and forgets the 1 percent prevalence that makes false positives dominate.
The prosecutor's fallacy confuses how unlikely the evidence is if innocent with how unlikely innocence is given the evidence.
In a city of ten million, about ten innocent people also match by chance, so a bare match makes the defendant only one in eleven.
The conjunction fallacy judges an intersection more probable than one of its parts, which the axioms forbid.
Every one of these is a failure to condition correctly, and writing the ratio down makes them impossible to commit.
